revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[hr:Avašima]][[ja:淡島神]][[ko:아와시마노카미]][[zh:淡島神]] {{Infobox deity}}
[[Image:Land Creation myths of Japan.svg|thumb|right|250px|Land Creation myth {{ill|Kuniumi|ja|国生み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuniumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国産み}} [[メディア:Land Creation myths of Japan.svg|View in SVG (compatible browsers only)]]]] '''Awashima-no-Kami''' (淡島神) is a deity ([[Kami]]) in Japanese {{ill|Folk belief|en|Folk belief|qid=Q28401154|lt=Folk belief|1=Folk belief}}. == Overview == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} and {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, '''Awashima''' appears as the second child born to {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}} during the {{ill|Kuniumi|en|Kuniumi|qid=Q3545163|lt=Kuniumi|1=Kuniumi}} (Creation of the Japanese Islands). However, like the first child, {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1067028}}, Awashima was born malformed and was set adrift in a reed boat, thus excluded from the official count of children. Although many places across Japan bear the name "Awashima," the specific location referenced in these texts is uncertain and has not been identified. Awashima-no-Kami is enshrined at {{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|淡嶋神社|1=Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|qq=}} and {{ill|Awaji Shrine|ja|淡路神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} nationwide, with the head shrine being {{ill|Awashima Shrine (Kada)|ja|淡嶋神社|1=Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine (Kada)|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine (Wakayama) (和歌山市加太)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7007362}} in Kada, Wakayama City, Wakayama Prefecture. However, during the Meiji-period {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, many shrines replaced Awashima-no-Kami with {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}. Temples dedicated to Awashima-no-Kami, known as '''Awashima-dō''', are also scattered throughout Japan. Awashima-no-Kami is particularly revered for healing women's illnesses, ensuring safe childbirth, fertility, improving sewing skills, and rituals for doll offerings. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, practitioners known as '''Awashima Gannin''' traveled the country carrying shrines dedicated to Awashima-no-Kami, preaching her virtues, spreading her worship widely. == Nature of Awashima-no-Kami == Various traditions describe the identity of Awashima-no-Kami (Awashima Myōjin): #Child of Izanagi and Izanami, as mentioned above. #Identified as {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}. Sukunabikona is revered as a deity of medicine, and according to the Nihon Shoki and {{ill|Hōki no Kuni Fudoki|ja|伯耆国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he departed from Awashima (identified with Awashima in Hōki Province) to the eternal land, {{ill|Tokoyo no kuni|en|Tokoyo no kuni|qid=Q11060259|lt=Tokoyo|1=Tokoyo no kuni}}. Many shrines, including Kada Awashima Shrine, adopt this belief, jointly enshrining Sukunabikona and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. #Awashima-no-Kami as the consort of {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}. Tradition says she was Amaterasu’s sixth child who married Sumiyoshi Myōjin but was banished to Awashima due to illness, vowing there to help women suffering similarly.<ref>{{ill|Tobe Tamio|ja|戸部民夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobe Tamio|1=Tamio Tobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q38277399}}, "Nihon no Kamigami: Tasai na Minzokushintachi" Shin-Kigensha, p.56.</ref> This belief is considered later folklore derived from the fact that Kada and neighboring {{ill|Tomogashima|en|Tomogashima|qid=Q7820112|lt=Tomogashima|1=Tomogashima}} were territories of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}.<ref name="神道大辞典">{{ill|Shintō Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11590924}}.</ref> #Identified with '''Barisaijo''' (婆利塞女). Related to the third tradition, local folklore states that at age 16 on the 3rd day of the 3rd month, Barisaijo dyed her teeth and married Sumiyoshi Myōjin (creating a sandbar passage from Ki-no-misaki in {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} to Sumiyoshi in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}). After contracting illness affecting her marriage, she made ritual dolls ({{ill|Katashiro|en|Katashiro|qid=Q11487867|lt=Katashiro|1=Katashiro}}) to remove the curse, hence the doll offerings at Kada Awashima Shrine.<ref name="神道大辞典"/> Another similarly named goddess, {{ill|Harisainyo|ja|頗梨采女|1=Harisai-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harisainyo|13=User:Immanuelle/Harisai-sho|qq=}}, is the consort of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|qid=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}}. The first and third traditions share the motif of "setting adrift in a boat," while the second similarly involves Sukunabikona arriving by boat from overseas. The third and fourth traditions emphasize Awashima as a goddess particularly helpful in curing women’s illnesses. Despite the gender associations, Kada Awashima Shrine enshrines {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}, known for aiding childbirth and healing diseases, thus the male deity tradition (Sukunabikona) does not diminish her protective role toward women. == Shrines and Temples Enshrining Awashima-no-Kami == [[File:Senso-ji Awashima-do 02.jpg|thumb|Sensō-ji Awashima-dō]] Many shrines dedicated to Awashima-no-Kami bear the name {{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|淡島（嶋）神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|粟島（嶋）神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Temples with Awashima-dō dedicated to Sukunabikona include: *Awashima-dō Sōtoku-ji (Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto) *Hachimanyama Jōkō-in {{ill|Shingan-ji (Setagaya)|ja|森巌寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingan-ji (Setagaya)|1=Shingan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16281450}} (Setagaya, Tokyo) *{{ill|Sensō-ji|en|Sensō-ji|qid=Q615183|lt=Sensō-ji|1=Sensō-ji}} Awashima-dō<ref>An image of Awashima Myōjin and her honjibutsu, {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}}, are enshrined here.</ref> (Taitō-ku, Tokyo) *{{ill|Miyajidake Shrine|en|Miyajidake Shrine|qid=Q3317296|lt=Miyajidake Shrine|1=Miyajidake Shrine}} Okunomiya Koi-no-Miya (Fukutsu City, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Hōryūzan Jōsen-ji (Midori-ku, Chiba City) == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == *Shintō Daijiten (Heibonsha, 1937; reprinted by Rinsen Shoten, 1969), ISBN 4-653-01347-0
== See Also == *{{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|淡島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine|qq=}} *{{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|粟島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine|qq=}} *{{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1067028}} *{{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|qid=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}} *{{ill|Hari-Kuyō|en|Hari-Kuyō|qid=Q1585326|lt=Needle Memorial Service|1=Hari-Kuyō}} {{Japanese mythology|qq=}} {{Shinto topics|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|淡島神|version=103602085|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[zh:淡島神]] [[ko:아와시마노카미]] [[hr:Avašima]] [[ja:淡島神]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q1575740}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:淡嶋神社]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]]
